skill.name: naming-analyzer
skill.description: Analyze identifier names in code and suggest clearer variable, function, class, file, and database names.
skill.summary: Review code naming conventions and recommend clearer, consistent identifiers.
skill.author: softaworks

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, environment, weak crypto, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found they are markdown examples and naming text. No executable files, network calls, secret collection, or prompt injection attempts were found in the reviewed files. The remaining risk is low because the skill can guide broad refactoring, which should stay under explicit user control.

low_findings.title: Markdown Naming Examples Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings are false positives from inline code formatting and fenced examples. The reviewed text shows naming conventions such as camelCase, PascalCase, snake_case, and sample identifiers, not shell execution or Ruby backticks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown documentation and code examples for naming style. There is no executable wrapper, shell invocation, or runtime script in the skill.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Keyword False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings do not correspond to hashing, encryption, or password handling logic. The cited lines contain normal prose, report labels, or naming examples rather than MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar implementations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no cryptographic API or algorithm use at these locations. The static detector appears to have matched substrings inside documentation text.

low_findings.title: Environment Access Appears Only in a Documentation Example
low_findings.description: The environment variable access finding is a code example for acceptable abbreviations. It does not instruct the model to read secrets, enumerate environment variables, or transmit configuration values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example is embedded in a markdown section about naming abbreviations. No surrounding instruction asks the model to access or expose environment data.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Findings Are Naming Text
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance findings are false positives from words such as env and generic naming examples. The reviewed lines do not request host inspection, system enumeration, or command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited context is about code style and identifier clarity. There is no semantic evidence of reconnaissance behavior.

low_findings.title: Refactoring Guidance Should Remain User Controlled
low_findings.description: The skill says it can generate refactoring scripts and suggests a fix-all mode. This is legitimate coding assistance, but broad renaming can affect many files and should require explicit user approval before edits run.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The capability is clearly documented and legitimate, but it could cause project-wide changes if used carelessly. No malicious automation or forced execution was found.

content.value_statement: Unclear names make code harder to read and maintain. This skill reviews identifiers and suggests clearer names based on language and project conventions.
actual_capabilities: Reviews variable, function, method, class, file, and directory names.
Checks common conventions for JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Java, and Go.
Flags vague, misleading, abbreviated, or inconsistent identifiers.
Suggests replacement names with short reasoning for each recommendation.
Groups naming issues by severity and priority for refactoring.
limitations: It relies on the code and context provided by the user.
It may need project standards to judge custom naming rules correctly.
It does not safely rename references by itself without tool support.
It may not infer domain meaning from very small snippets.

use_cases.title: Review Pull Request Naming
use_cases.description: Find unclear or misleading identifiers before code review is complete.

use_cases.title: Standardize Legacy Code
use_cases.description: Identify inconsistent naming styles across older files before refactoring work starts.

use_cases.title: Teach Naming Conventions
use_cases.description: Explain why names should follow language conventions and project patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Review One File
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a small code sample.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the naming in this file. List unclear names, explain the issue, and suggest better names.

prompt_templates.title: Check Language Conventions
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this code for naming convention issues. Use the standard conventions for the language in the file.

prompt_templates.title: Prioritize Refactoring
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this directory for naming issues. Group recommendations by high, medium, and low priority.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Naming Standard
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these files and infer the project naming style. Propose a concise naming standard with exceptions and migration steps.

output_examples.input: A user service has a read method that also updates login state.
output_examples.output: The report marks the method name as misleading, explains the side effect, and suggests a name that includes both fetching and updating.

output_examples.input: A component uses short names such as data, temp, and x across several blocks.
output_examples.output: The report flags vague identifiers and suggests names tied to the values role, scope, and domain meaning.

output_examples.input: A codebase mixes snake case, camel case, and uppercase constants in the same language.
output_examples.output: The report identifies convention drift and recommends one project style with priority groups for cleanup.

best_practices: Provide enough surrounding code so suggestions match real behavior.
Share project naming rules when they differ from language defaults.
Use IDE rename tools or tests when applying broad naming changes.
anti_patterns: Do not rename identifiers from tiny snippets without checking references.
Do not prefer short names when they hide domain meaning.
Do not apply every suggestion if it conflicts with project standards.

faq.question: What does this skill review?
faq.answer: It reviews identifier names, naming conventions, clarity, consistency, and misleading names in code.

faq.question: Can it rename files automatically?
faq.answer: It can suggest changes, but automatic renaming should use explicit user approval and safe refactoring tools.

faq.question: Which languages are covered?
faq.answer: The documentation covers JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Java, Go, and general naming patterns.

faq.question: Does it understand project-specific conventions?
faq.answer: It can follow project patterns when enough examples or written rules are provided.

faq.question: Can it help with boolean names?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends prefixes such as is, has, can, and should when they fit the meaning.

faq.question: Is this a security scanning skill?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on naming quality, readability, and maintainability.
